content:
Welcome back, this is lesson 6.2 of January 2017 ICT Mentorship, dealing with trade conditions and setup
progression. We will be outlining the 1200 pip move mentioned in the money management lesson number
five, serving as a brief recap and refresher.

When looking at a premium market, and markets are trading near a premium, we suspect lower future
prices. We're looking for displacement in price. Conversely, this example focuses on a buying opportunity
when a market goes to a deep discount on monthly, weekly, and daily time frames, using that insight to
target a higher premium future price.

This teaching specifically deals with buying at a discount and aiming for premium objectives. Our focus for
entries on long positions, down at discount, includes the following bullish PD Arrays:
- Mitigation blocks
- Bullish Breakers
- Liquidity Voids
- Fair Value Gaps
- Bullish Order Blocks
- Rejection Blocks
- Old Lows or Old Highs
The idea is to look for something in this list of arrays to target one of the premium arrays.

section_title: PROGRESSION OF TARGETING PREMIUM OBJECTIVES FROM DISCOUNT
content:
The first objective will be a mitigation block. If there's a breaker, we would aim for that, considering if there's
significant force to drive price through it. We also look for any range to be filled in for a liquidity void, aiming
for fair value gaps in the premium range of the market. Then we look at bearish order blocks, rejection
blocks, and old highs or old historic lows. We're looking for bullish PD arrays to buy into with the
expectation of selling at one of the monthly premium arrays.

If we were looking at a bearish example, we would be looking to sell short at a bearish PD array, aiming to
cover that short position with one of the bullish PD arrays, which include:
- Mitigation block
- Bullish Breaker
- Liquidity Void
- Fair Value Gap
- Bullish Order Block
- Rejection Block
- Old Low or Historic High
This describes selling at a premium and buying back at a discount.

Before getting into the example, it's important to keep some study points in mind. When markets are at a
premium or discount, they will always initially look to rebalance to the equilibrium price point between the
last recent range. Even if it doesn't go all the way to a deep premium or discount, you can always
reasonably expect it to go back to equilibrium. If you buy at a real deep discount and just aim to get back to
equilibrium, you'll find a lot of trades like that. If you're at a big premium (historic, annual, or three-month
high), you have a good chance of seeing the market sell off back to some equilibrium price point of the
most recent trading range.

When markets are in premium and if you're in equilibrium, you're going to be focusing on the market
potentially moving up into one of these monthly PD arrays that possibly may be a shorting opportunity.
When the market's at equilibrium, you can anticipate a market move down into a monthly discount, aiming
for one of the PD arrays as an objective. So, at equilibrium, you can look for either expansion to the upside
or downside. You'll be consulting the market structure on the monthly chart to determine what side of the
marketplace you're going to be looking for; it's not indiscriminate.

section_title: PD ARRAYS ACROSS TIMEFRAMES & JAPANESE YEN CASH PRICE INTRODUCTION
content:
Weekly ranges and daily charts utilize the same PD arrays for premium and discount. We will now move
through the charts and look at the Japanese Yen cash price. This is the daily cash price for Japanese Yen,
seen at barchart.com. Historically, in 2016, a high was made in August, and the market showed a
willingness to break down market structure at a low. This low was broken, so market structure on a long
- term basis on a daily chart had been broken bearishly. We observed the Trump election rally up into a
bearish order block, and then it sold off. We'll be looking at this specific move.

section_title: JAPANESE YEN CASH PRICE VS. USD/JPY
content:
To understand clearly, especially for new traders used to Futures or Japanese Yen cash price, when we
pair it with a currency like the Dollar Index, the Dollar Index is the front currency and the Japanese Yen is
the second. This means if we're bearish or expecting lower prices on Japanese Yen cash prices, the USD/
JPY pair will actually be inverted; it's going to go up. If Japanese Yen cash price is dropping, that means
Dollar prices are rallying, and if the Dollar is first in the pair name (USD/JPY), then you're watching the
advancement of the Dollar versus the decline of the Japanese Yen.

USD/JPY
- OVERVIEW AND DISCOUNT IDENTIFICATION
Here is a monthly chart of the USD/JPY. You can see a very nice rally up in here, which is diametrically
opposed to what we expected or just saw on the Japanese Yen cash price. As a reminder, we discussed
the Japanese Yen in earlier months of the mentorship, including levels like 118. We will now map out this
monthly chart. We identify a high and then equal lows where sell stops would be expected. While the
market was dropping last year, we expected it to trade down into these levels. These two down candles
blend together to form a bullish order block, giving an equilibrium price point. We'll round it to the full 99
level, establishing a premium high end and a discount low end. This becomes a discount level because
price moved away from it, leaving a gap where price was only delivered on the upside, with no down
movement to counteract. Price came down, closing that small range, and hit the big figure 99.

The 99 level represents the discount on the monthly chart. We would look to see a move below the wicks
and bodies of these candles, which occurred in November 2016. The candle went below the bodies of the
candles, which constitutes a rejection block. It cleared out the bodies on a monthly chart, rejected, and sent
price higher.

We also have a range up here and a down candle that acts as a breaker. This is a breaker because we had
an old high where short sellers would place buy stops. The market rallied above, cleared out those buy
stops, and then traded down through it. Once this down candle is violated, it becomes a valid breaker. If
price trades down below it and then comes back to its middle body, extending that out in time would give a
level to watch (this is actually a weekly level, which will be clearer on the weekly chart). Additionally, there
is a gap where price was only delivered on the downside between a candle's low and a subsequent
candle's high. This gap would be a reasonable objective when price was rallying.

Here's the same USD/JPY in a weekly format. We have an up candle right before the down move, which is
a bearish order block. We map its opening out. We use the open of this candle, not the wick, because there
is an absence of a gap (Fair Value Gap). This area had a wick and was traded down through, so it was
passed through two times, and blocked in by this candle's high. This entire area would not be viewed as a
gap or Fair Value Gap, as it's been closed in because the bearish order block itself had an initial movement
lower.

section_title: FAIR VALUE GAP AND BULLISH ORDER BLOCK (USD/JPY)
content:
Price came up and closed right to the opening. We also have a gap here, a fair value gap, between this
candle's low and this candle's high, which would also be an objective to reach for. On the weekly chart, we
are deep in a discount on the range, and price shows a willingness to rally. It comes back down into the
down candle right before that move; this is a bullish order block. You could be a buyer here, but we're
focusing on that November low. This move is trading right back into and recapitalizing an old weekly bullish
order block. Price hit that perfectly and rallied away.

section_title: BEARISH ORDER BLOCK AND REJECTION BLOCKS (USD/JPY)
content:
On a weekly timeframe, this bearish order block (the last up candle right before the down move) is what
you could have on your charts as a reasonable objective once price comes down and hits it. Once it's hit,
this wouldn't be resistance whatsoever. So, you can take that level and move it over. That would be a
reasonable objective on the upside, closing in that range. Now, look at the equal body candles; above that,
we would expect to see a rejection block. Price reaches through that as well; another rejection block
candidate here with equal body candles. We can expect to see that as an upside objective as price
continues to trade higher.

section_title: FINAL WEEKLY OBJECTIVES AND TRANSITION TO DAILY CHART
content:
Further up, we have that fair value gap where price was only delivered on the downside, which would be
another objective. And finally, all the way up to the bearish order block, which is the last up candle before
the down move. So, we have a discount to premium progression using the monthly and weekly charts. Now,
let's go down into a daily timeframe.

section_title: LAYERING DAILY BULLISH ORDER BLOCK ON WEEKLY CHART
content:
After adding one more thing, we also have this last down candle right before this buy occurred. We're going
to map that out with a different color so we can see the hierarchy between the weekly and the daily. Going
over to a daily chart.

section_title: INTERACTION WITH WEEKLY BULLISH ORDER BLOCKS
content:
On the daily chart, the darker area represents a weekly bullish order block. This is the lower weekly order
block, and this is the higher weekly order block. Price comes down and actually touches both, giving a
return back to the lower weekly order block, which is bullish. So, it trades two times into a higher order
block on the weekly chart and the lower of the two bullish order blocks. Price then rallies away and through
during this November buy.

On the daily chart, we also see an old high here, an old high here, and equal highs. We have relatively
equal highs as well. We have a fair value gap. This high and this low would have been on the weekly chart.
And then we have the last up candle right here, with a low at 118.55. So, 118.55, you can see how prices
reached up into a premium level.

section_title: IMPORTANCE OF DOWN CANDLES IN AN UPTREND
content:
An important thing is that if we don't see any retracement back into a daily bullish order block, or if we saw,
for instance, this down candle right here, price rallies away from it. Every time you look at an uptrend, you
want to focus on the down candles because that's where institutions are going to buy, either they're buying
at the time of the down...
